author: @williamfraser
text: You should get better performance if your pulley is less heat conductive and your nichrome wire is arranged in discrete coils (each around a radial axis, like the stator of a brushless motor). It would localise the heat, allowing faster loss and recovery of magnetism. This would result in a better defined boundary between hot and cold coils and a stronger tangential force from the magnet. I knew there was a reason for not throwing away that old toaster...

ACTUALLY I have SEVERAL toasters, but I'm saving them for a novel electric oven project for baking/curing powder coated car parts....

But also you can even get nickel welding rods or old junked out heaters, [or as you mentioned hair dryers, (even clothes dryers, as those things have a LOT of it!)] so a lot of nickel indeed could be found.

I suggest that the nickel be put up in segments if only to prevent heat loss, so therefore, you can mount pieces of nickel in sticks, coils, buttons or tabs so that your heat can wash over them at just the right locations...

Also, you can heat multiple points and use multiple magnets for more torque at any speed.

author: @CreativeCircuits
text:
+ Robert
You could also use Nitinol  (Nickel Titanium) wire and have it rotate through a water bath on the opposite side of the heat source or hot water bath. This is much easier to rotate as the Nitinol is much more reactive to heating and cooling and takes less heat, subsequently not causing damage due to excessive heat. The Nitinol wire is more like a motor in itself as it can be made in a coil and then when heat is applied it straitens and when cool is applied it returns to it's original shape. It does show tremendous promise and there have been some good studies into this and some machines have been made to do actual work, but for some reason it never went to a more developed stage.Nitinol will not wear out but in fact it actually gets stronger over repetition, of which this phenomenon is yet to be understood . 

Thermal energy is the most abundant energy source we have and it is the most under utilized. I have done numerous experiments in this area and have made some awesome progress in projects that most certainly can be made for individual home use to provide a self sustained home energy source for everyone to be independent of metered power or money based systems. We can easily store heat energy and we have an abundance of it all around us in many different forms from a constant supply to all of our waste heat. You can use the Nitinol on a low thermal source as a low thermal device and this heat can be stored in vegetable oil and used when there is no constant source such as solar thermal in the day. Vegetable oil is a great medium to store heat and can achieve 450f heat and store this heat in thermally insulated tanks for months at a time.
Nitinol can work on as little as a 20 degree thermal difference which can easily be achieved and can power and do large work in kilowatts and scaled up to even do megawatts of work.

"Vegetable oil is a great medium to store heat and can achieve 450f heat and store this heat in thermally insulated tanks for months at a time." thats very interesting. I have been experimenting with plastic jugs in front of a window in the wintertime. releases the heat at night.


"Nitinol can work on as little as a 20 degree thermal difference which can easily be achieved " yeah thats the great thing about nitinol. needs such a small heat differential to do serious mechanical work in a small space.

replies.author: @CreativeCircuits
replies.text:
@yougeo Haha, Awesome! You keep experimenting. The great thing about veg oil is that you can make it yourself to continue in true self sufficiency. It has a high heat storage capability and can be used with a heat exchange to boil water and make steam and cook and bake food and as a home heating medium. It stays in a closed loop system and you never consume the oil. You can heat the oil with any heat source, waste heat or solar which is most abundant and no work or if needed during no sun it can be supplement heated with another source such as a gasifier that burns trash and turns that trash into fuel that also can be stored. The oil is good for a few years at constant use before it loses efficiency and can be replaced without money if you continue growing crops and saving seeds, regrowing crops and pressing oil.


Nitinol has a tremendous force of 5 tons per square inch and this metal should be used in energy harvesting machines along with the awesome metal Gadolinium (Gd) which is also highly reactive to temperature change except it rapidly turns on and off high strength magnetism.

author: @lubricatedgoat
text:
Very cool!  Can you try this metal?  Cu2MnAl  (read the Curie point is 97C).
Make a wheel wrapped in Cu2MnAl that dips into boiling water.  Magnet can be placed in the water.
It could be used as a pump :)
As long as the water is boiling, or at least 97C, the wheel should turn.  Might make it easy to control temperature since the water will never exceed 100C.
The arrangement would also allow the alloy and the magnet to be in extremely close proximity (underwater) thus greatly increasing the magnetic attraction.  The wheel could also be extremely light, even plastic.
I actually wonder how efficient this would be!?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: would do if i had some, mate - but i'm looking into getting some - cheers

replies.author: @lubricatedgoat
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering This actually has me thinking some more.

Using an alloy with a Curie point to match the temperature of waste heat from, say, a power plant, or other low-grade heat, one could construct the following:
A vacuum (maybe a box section) chamber with IR transparency could house lightweight flywheels (carbon fiber or plastic) along its length.
The lower side of the chamber, about the width of the flywheel, would have hot-spots designed to radiate heat to the alloy on the wheel.
The wheels are attached to generators (inside the vacuum).
The thin, and presumably black-coated alloy on the wheels rapidly heats as it passes over the radiative heat source, going above the Curie point, and the rest we know.  The alloy then has the rest of its cycle to cool off until it's under the transition temperature.
I'm wondering if a system like this, thermodynamically, could compete with low temperature differential thermal engines, like Stirling or Kalina.
It would have extremely low friction, and the alloy tape thickness, magnet position, wheel diameter etc., could all be optimized.
Be interesting to test or simulate.
Perhaps it would make a good simple generator with real-world applications.
A larger version could use a liquid metal to conduct heat to the flywheel, to an alloy that is the equivalent of hydrophobic to the metal.  This way the wheel, being large, could spin more slowly and not have (much) frictional losses due to stirring the liquid metal, yet have great gains in heat transfer and simplicity of design.
Could the alloy be made of something that is actually magnetic?  Like a ring of small magnets that lose their magnetism when heated, and a larger stationary magnet below that is not affected by the heat.  This way the system could be designed to attract magnets coming down, then repel them going up as they regain magnetism using another optimally placed stationary magnet.
Sorry for the long rant.

author: @darthfate2139
text: In Weller soldering pen is used this effect of Curie temperature for precise temperature control. It is magnet inside pen heated by some heating element that magnet other side touches soldering nedle. Next of it is spring loaded electric contactor. Its called MAGNASTAT. Similar system can be used with crankshaft and piston rod. At end of piston rod can be piece of nickel aloy,.....

replies.author: @yougeo
replies.text: that is very clever! magnastat
